Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty loose. I'd set aside time on weekends mostly, maybe 4-5 hours at a stretch, and then squeeze in study sessions whenever I could during the week. Being 29 and juggling other responsibilities, I couldn't always stick to a perfect schedule. I'm a visual learner, so I'd spend a lot of time with diagrams and charts, sometimes making my own from the lecture content. The 26 hours I put in weekly were scattered but I made them count when I could focus.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on what was due. Some weeks I'd be pretty consistent, logging in every other day, but when deadlines piled up I'd cram more intensely. Life stuff would throw me off too—work obligations, errands, the usual chaos of being an older student. I tried to have some structure but honestly, flexibility was more realistic for my situation.
Q3: Usually it was checking if there were new assignments posted or seeing if grades came back. The gamified tasks actually got me logging in more often because I wanted to see my progress and earn those badges or points. Sometimes I'd just pop in to watch a video segment I'd been meaning to finish. Notifications helped too, though I didn't always act on them immediately.
Q4: Yeah, after a few weeks the gamified elements made it almost habit-forming. I'd check in to see leaderboards or complete small challenges even when nothing was strictly due. It became like a quick morning check, almost like scrolling through social media but actually productive. The routine stuck because there was always something small to accomplish.
Q5: The video lectures were gold for me since I learn best visually. I could pause, rewind, and really absorb the diagrams and demonstrations. The gamified tasks also kept me engaged because they broke down material into bite-sized challenges that felt achievable. Discussion boards were useful too when people posted helpful summaries or visual aids.
Q6: I'll admit I skipped a lot of the lengthy reading PDFs. With limited resources and time, I prioritized what gave me the most visual payoff. Some of the optional supplementary materials I just never got to—not because they weren't valuable, but because I had to triage. If it was text-heavy without visuals, it usually got pushed aside.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate progress or if it connected clearly to assignments and exams. The gamified elements helped a lot here—earning points or completing a challenge gave instant feedback. If something felt like busywork without a clear purpose, I'd deprioritize it fast.
Q8: Optional activities felt like bonuses rather than obligations. I'd do them when I was in the mood or had extra time, which wasn't often. Required ones had that weight of consequences attached, so they got done first. But honestly, some optional gamified tasks were more engaging than required readings, so I'd sometimes do those instead.
Q9: Absolutely. I'd download lecture slides and study them on my phone during commutes or breaks. I made my own visual notes and diagrams on paper since that helps things stick for me. Sometimes I'd find YouTube videos that explained concepts better than the course materials. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the screen.
Q10: There were plenty of times I'd be reviewing my handwritten notes or working through problems on paper for hours, and none of that would show up anywhere. I'd also discuss concepts with a friend who took a similar course before—just talking through ideas helped solidify my understanding. The LMS only saw me when I was actively clicking around.
Q11: All the time. I'd watch a video once, download the slides, and then study from those offline for days. My internet isn't always reliable, so having materials saved locally was essential. I'd revisit those downloaded files repeatedly but the system would only register that initial access.
Q12: Mid-semester got rough when multiple assignments overlapped and I was dealing with some financial stress. My attendance dropped and I felt disconnected from the course for a couple weeks. The gamified tasks actually helped pull me back because they were low-stakes ways to re-engage without feeling overwhelmed.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or stressed about money, the LMS felt like another burden. I'd log in but just skim through things without really processing. Competing responsibilities meant the course sometimes got whatever energy I had left, which wasn't always much. Fatigue made even simple tasks feel harder.
Q14: Yeah, there were sessions where I'd have videos playing but my mind was elsewhere. I'd technically be 'watching' but not absorbing anything. Sometimes I'd click through readings just to mark them complete without really engaging. Those moments felt like going through the motions rather than actual learning.
Q15: I procrastinated on the longer written assignments because they felt daunting. I knew they mattered for my grade but I'd keep pushing them back until the deadline forced my hand. It wasn't rational—I'd stress about them while avoiding them. The gamified smaller tasks were easier to tackle, so I'd do those instead.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my external structure. Without them, I'd drift. I'd ramp up my LMS activity significantly in the days before something was due, cramming in videos and readings I'd postponed. The system probably showed spikes of activity clustered around due dates rather than steady engagement.
Q17: When I got feedback, especially positive feedback, it motivated me to engage more. Seeing that my assignment scores were solid made me feel like my approach was working. Negative or confusing feedback sometimes made me hesitant to engage further until I figured out what went wrong. Quick feedback loops kept me more connected.
Q18: The course structure with clear modules and the gamified elements gave me a roadmap. I knew what was expected each week, which helped me plan despite my chaotic schedule. The points and badges created mini-goals that made the larger course feel more manageable. Without that structure, I probably would have drifted more.
Q19: The gamification definitely made engagement easier—it turned studying into something with visible rewards. What made it harder was when resources were text-heavy without visual components. Also, some discussion requirements felt forced rather than organic. The flexibility in pacing helped accommodate my irregular schedule though.
Q20: I think those students might be exploring, trying to understand the material by watching everything, but then struggling to translate that into submitted work. Maybe they're dealing with perfectionism or anxiety about their output. Or they could be like me sometimes—engaging with content but running out of time before assignments are due.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat. I didn't have the highest LMS activity but I studied a lot offline and still passed. You can learn effectively without constantly being logged in. Someone who knows how to study efficiently might grab what they need and work independently. The LMS doesn't capture everything.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, how long you spend. But they miss the quality of that engagement and all the offline learning. They can't measure understanding or effort put into handwritten notes. They see the surface behavior, not the thinking happening underneath.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm curious about a concept and dig deeper, or when I'm actively thinking rather than passively consuming. True engagement is mental investment, and that doesn't always look like high activity metrics.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I relied on visual learning strategies outside the platform—my hand-drawn diagrams, offline review sessions, conversations with others. It wouldn't capture the times I was mentally engaged while doing something that didn't require clicks. And it definitely wouldn't show how the gamified elements actually shifted my motivation and made me feel more connected to the course.
